one_line_takeaway: By passively sniffing only encrypted Wi-Fi packet-size/timing metadata at the access point, FLARE fingerprints whether a federated-learning client is training a CNN or RNN with up to ~99% F1 in closed-world and ~88-91% F1 in open-world settings, and shows this fingerprint can be weaponized to make a resource-denial attack against CNN clients 3-4x more disruptive.
key_finding: In the closed-world setting, FLARE fusion is near-perfect: for CNN-vs-Rest, MetaLR fusion reaches precision 0.985 +/- 0.017, recall 1.000 +/- 0.000, F1 0.992 +/- 0.008 (vs. flow-only F1 0.963 +/- 0.016 and packet-only F1 0.894 +/- 0.024); for RNN-vs-Rest, MetaXGB fusion reaches precision 1.000 +/- 0.000, recall 0.963 +/- 0.050, F1 0.980 +/- 0.027 (vs. flow-only F1 0.946 +/- 0.057, packet-only F1 0.688 +/- 0.148). In the harder open-world setting (unseen architectures at test time), fusion still generalizes: CNN-vs-Rest MetaLR fusion F1 0.914 +/- 0.070 (packet-only 0.587 +/- 0.185, flow-only 0.929 +/- 0.071), RNN-vs-Rest MetaXGB fusion F1 0.879 +/- 0.074 (packet-only 0.554 +/- 0.323, flow-only 0.917 +/- 0.144) -- fusion consistently reduces variance versus single-view models even where it does not top raw F1. The paper identifies a practical sniffing window of 250-600s (F1 improves from 60s to 240s, is stable 240-420s, and stays close to 80% out to 600s before degrading further from feature-smoothing). Fisher-score, KL-divergence (Table II: e.g., Jetson Orin client shows mean-frame KL divergence 8.70 +/- 1.63 CNN-referenced vs. 0.49 +/- 0.14 RNN-referenced; mean-IAT KL 12.73 +/- 0.60 vs. 2.75 +/- 3.80), and t-SNE/K-means clustering analyses corroborate that inter-arrival time and packet size are the most discriminative features. In the resource-denial downstream experiment, successful fingerprinting enables selectively throttling two-thirds of throughput to a client and delays convergence by 109% (single-client, sync FL) and 319% (multi-client, async FL) for CNN targets versus 20% and 19% for RNN targets; misclassifying the architecture before attacking substantially blunts the attack (e.g., a misclassified RNN attack still increases delay but at ~167% higher throttling cost than a correctly classified attack).
core_contribution: FLARE (FL Architecture REconnaissance) is a passive, wireless side-channel fingerprinting attack that infers the broad deep learning architecture family (CNN vs. RNN) being trained by a federated learning (FL) client, using only encrypted 802.11 Wi-Fi traffic metadata sniffed at the access point. The pipeline segments traffic into fixed windows (250-300s), extracts flow-level features (packet-rate statistics, directional packet-size statistics) and packet-level features (packet-length histograms, edge/first-last packet size and inter-arrival-time statistics), trains independent Random Forest one-vs-rest base classifiers on each feature view, and fuses their output probabilities via a late-fusion meta-classifier (logistic regression 'MetaLR' or XGBoost 'MetaXGB'). The paper also demonstrates a downstream consequence: using the fingerprint to selectively throttle bandwidth (a resource-denial attack) more effectively than an attacker without architecture knowledge.
threat_model: A passive, non-participating adversary sits at (or controls/observes) the Wi-Fi access point that is wired to the FL server, e.g. a compromised router, passive eavesdropper on the wireless link, or honest-but-curious edge node. The adversary can sniff all uplink/downlink 802.11 traffic between clients and server and extract side-channel metadata (packet size, direction, timestamps, inter-arrival time, burst patterns) but cannot decrypt payloads, cannot access model parameters/gradients/training data, and does not inject, modify, or drop packets or otherwise interfere with the FL protocol. MAC address randomization is assumed disabled (so a MAC address maps to one client), each client trains only one FL model per session, and the adversary lacks higher-layer protocol information (e.g., TCP sequence numbers). The attack goal is a multi-class/one-vs-rest classification: from a sniffed traffic window, predict whether the client is training a CNN or an RNN (other traffic is 'unknown'/Rest).
limitations: The paper explicitly frames this as broad architecture-family (CNN vs. RNN) fingerprinting only -- fine-grained intra-family distinctions (e.g., ResNet vs. DenseNet) are noted as 'less pronounced' in the t-SNE/clustering analysis (Section V-D) and are not solved. Evaluation uses only three physical client devices, so device-diversity coverage is limited. The threat model assumes MAC randomization is disabled and one model per client session -- both are stated simplifying assumptions rather than universal deployment realities. The open-world results show substantially higher variance and lower precision/recall than closed-world (e.g., open-world CNN-vs-Rest MetaXGB flow-only precision drops to 0.567 +/- 0.361), indicating real deployment performance would be less reliable than the closed-world headline numbers. No comparison to any prior baseline attack is possible or attempted since the authors state this is the first work on FL architecture fingerprinting via wireless side channels -- so the reported numbers are self-relative (fusion vs. single-view ablations) rather than benchmarked against an external method. Proposed countermeasures (packet padding, randomized update schedules, secure aggregation, sparsification/quantization, MAC randomization) are discussed only qualitatively in Section VI-B, with tradeoffs noted (bandwidth/latency cost, synchronization overhead, potential FL performance degradation under non-IID data) but none are implemented or evaluated empirically against FLARE.

datasets_used: UCI HAR (Human Activity Recognition, smartphone sensor time series)
quantitative_results: Closed-world CNN-vs-Rest, MetaLR fusion: precision 0.985 +/- 0.017, recall 1.000 +/- 0.000, F1 0.992 +/- 0.008
Closed-world CNN-vs-Rest, flow-only F1 0.963 +/- 0.016; packet-only F1 0.894 +/- 0.024
Closed-world RNN-vs-Rest, MetaXGB fusion: precision 1.000 +/- 0.000, recall 0.963 +/- 0.050, F1 0.980 +/- 0.027
Closed-world RNN-vs-Rest, flow-only F1 0.946 +/- 0.057; packet-only F1 0.688 +/- 0.148
Open-world CNN-vs-Rest, MetaLR fusion F1 0.914 +/- 0.070 (recall 1.000 +/- 0.000, precision 0.850 +/- 0.122); packet-only F1 0.587 +/- 0.185; flow-only F1 0.929 +/- 0.071
Open-world RNN-vs-Rest, MetaXGB fusion: precision 1.00 +/- 0.00, recall 0.792 +/- 0.125, F1 0.879 +/- 0.074; packet-only F1 0.554 +/- 0.323; flow-only F1 0.917 +/- 0.144
Practical sniffing window identified as 250-600s; F1 stays close to 80% out to 600s before further degradation

KL divergence (Table II), Jetson Orin client (C0), CNN-referenced: mean frame 8.70 +/- 1.63, mean IAT 12.73 +/- 0.60, std IAT 12.64 +/- 2.19; RNN-referenced: mean frame 0.49 +/- 0.14, mean IAT 2.75 +/- 3.80, std IAT 5.30 +/- 3.91
Uncompromised mean effective throughput: CNN 216 +/- 192 Mbps, RNN 384 +/- 56 Mbps
Resource-denial convergence delay increase after successful fingerprinting: CNN single-client (sync) +109%, CNN multi-client (async) +319%; RNN single-client +20%, RNN multi-client +19%
Misclassified-target attack cost: misclassified RNN attack costs ~167% more (in throttling) than a correctly classified attack to achieve comparable delay
baselines_compared: No external prior-work baseline exists (paper states it is the first FL architecture-fingerprinting-via-wireless-side-channel work); comparisons are internal ablations: flow-only Random Forest vs. packet-only Random Forest vs. late-fusion (FLARE) with MetaLR vs. MetaXGB meta-classifiers
